perturbation.original_text:
4.  Terminations of Services
Upon the termination of the Recipient for the Purpose;
4.1.1 The Recipient will, on request from the Discloser, return all copies and records of the Confidential Information to the Discloser and will not retain any copies or records of the Confidential Information; and
4.1.2 The Recipient shall make no further use of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text:
4.  Terminations of Services
Upon the termination of the Recipient for the Purpose;
4.1.1 The Recipient will, on request from the Discloser, return all copies and records of the Confidential Information to the Discloser within 90 days of the request and will not retain any copies or records of the Confidential Information; and
4.1.2 The Recipient shall make no further use of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Imposing a 90-day deadline for returning confidential information after termination is an unreasonably long duration that contradicts standard practices. It does not make business sense to allow 90 days, as most companies expect a much quicker return of materials. There should be immediate action or it would be considered negligent.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singapore Personal Data Protection Act (PDPA) - Section 26(2)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 26(2) of the PDPA requires organizations to cease retention of documents containing personal data or remove the means by which the personal data can be associated with particular individuals as soon as it is reasonable to assume that the purpose for which that personal data was collected is no longer being served by retention of the personal data and retention is no longer necessary for legal or business purposes. Giving 90 days to return confidential data after termination is too long to adhere to this requirement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: PDPC | Page Not Found Search Toggle search bar Toggle mobile menu Search Be aware of scammers impersonating as PDPC officers and report any suspicious calls to the Police. Do not reveal personal information over the phone to unknown parties. You may call PDPC (6377 3131) to verify the identity of the alleged PDPC officer. For scam-related advice, visit www.scamshield.gov.sg . Close notification The PDPC website has recently undergone a transformation. Please click the links below if you are looking for: Overview of the Personal Data Protection Act (PDPA) Commission's Decisions Advisory on Collection of Personal Data for COVID-19 Contact Tracing Advisory Guidelines Help for DP Professionals E-Learning Programme DPO Connect e-Newsletter Take me back to homepage . Share To offer you a better experience, this site uses cookies. Read more about cookies in our Privacy Statement. Close Back to top of page Personal Data Protection Commission Contact Us FAQs Sitemap © 2025 Personal Data Protection Commission Last updated 05 Dec 2023
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 90-day delay for returning confidential information, which the Law Explanation argues conflicts with Section 26(2) of the PDPA. The Law Explanation states that the PDPA requires organizations to cease retention of personal data as soon as it is reasonable to assume that the purpose for which that personal data was collected is no longer being served. The Perturbation Explanation also supports this, stating that a 90-day period is an unreasonably long duration that contradicts standard practices. Although the scraped snippets do not contain the explicit law, snippet 2 makes reference to the Personal Data Protection Commission (PDPC) and their website; which is where one would presumably find details about the PDPA. Given the Law Explanation's reference to a specific section of the PDPA, as well as the Perturbation Explanation, the 90-day delay likely creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text:
3.1.1 any information which is or in future comes into the public domain (unless as a result of the breach of this Agreement); or any information which is already known to the Recipient and which was not subject to any obligation of confidence before it was disc losed to the Recipient by the Discloser.
3.1.2
perturbation.changed_text:
3.1.1 any information which is or in future comes into the public domain (regardless of whether as a result of the breach of this Agreement); or any information which is already known to the Recipient and which was not subject to any obligation of confidence before it was disc losed to the Recipient by the Discloser.
3.1.2
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the agreement to state that information entering the public domain is no longer considered confidential, 'regardless of whether' the information was released due to a breach of contract, directly contradicts the standard interpretation and enforcement of NDAs. The clause implies the recipient will not be held responsible for breaches of confidentiality.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singapore Law of Contract - Breach of Contract Remedies
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the exception for breaches, the modified clause seeks to absolve the Recipient from liability even if their actions cause the information to become public. This directly violates the principle that a party breaching a contract is liable for damages resulting from that breach. Singapore contract law, like most common law systems, allows for remedies, including damages, for breach of contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Ch. 08 The Law of Contract www.singaporelawwatch.sg COMMERCIAL LAW Search Ch. 08 The Law of Contract Ch. 08A Remedies for Breach of Contract Ch. 09 Domestic Sale of Goods Ch. 10 International Sale of Goods Ch. 11 The Law of Credit and Security Ch. 12 Intellectual Property Law Ch. 13 Intellectual Property Licensing Ch. 14 Forms of Business Organisations Ch. 15 Law of Agency Ch. 16 Singapore Company Law Ch. 17 Corporate Finance and Securities Regulation Ch. 18 Equity and Trusts Ch. 19 Restitution Ch. 20 The Law of Negligence Ch. 21 Economic Torts Ch. 22 Banking and Finance Ch. 23 The Law of Guarantees Ch. 24 Insurance Law Ch. 25 Shipping Law Ch. 26 Building and Construction Law Ch. 27 Competition Law Ch. 28 Income Taxation Ch. 29 Land Law Ch. 30 Bankruptcy and Insolvency Ch. 31 Technology, Media, and Telecom No content A problem occurred while loading content. Previous Next Ch. 08 The Law of Contract SECTION 1 GENERAL APPLICATION A. Singapore contract law largely based on English contract law 8.1.1 Contract law in Singapore is largely based on the common law of contract in England. Hence, the rules developed in the Singapore courts do bear a very close resemblance to those developed under English common law. Indeed, where there is no Singapore authority specifically on point, it will usually be assumed that the position will, in the first instance, be no different from that in England. Unlike its neighbours Malaysia and Brunei, following Independence in 1965, Singapore’s Parliament made no attempt to codify Singapore’s law of contract. Accordingly, much of the law of contract in Singapore remains in the form of judge-made rules. B. Certain aspects of Singapore contract law based on specific statutes 8.1.2 In some circumstances, these judge-made rules have been modified by specific statutes. Many of these statutes are English in origin. To begin with, 13 English commercial statutes have been incorporated as part of the Statutes of the Republic of Singapore by virtue of s 4 of the Application of English Law Act (Cap 7A, 1993 Rev Ed). These are listed in Part II of the First Schedule of this Act. Other statutes, eg the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act (Cap 53B, 2002 Rev Ed), are modelled upon English statutes. There are also other areas where statutory development based on non-English models has taken place, eg the Consumer Protection (Fair Trading) Act (Cap 52A, 2004 Rev Ed) (which was largely drawn from fair trading legislation enacted in Alberta and Sasketchewan). SECTION 2 OFFER AND ACCEPTANCE A. Contract is essentially agreement between two or more parties 8.2.1 A contract is essentially an agreement between two or more parties, the terms of which affect their respective rights and obligations which are enforceable at law. Whether the parties have reached agreement, or a meeting of the minds, is objectively ascertained from the facts. The concepts of offer and acceptance provide in many, albeit not all, cases the starting point for analysing whether agreement has been reached. B. Offer 1. Definition of offer 8.2.2 An offer is a promise, or other expression of willingness, by the ‘offeror’ to be bound on certain specified terms upon the unqualified acceptance of these terms by the person to whom the offer is made (the ‘offeree’). Provided the other formation elements (ie consideration and intention to create legal relations) are present, the acceptance of an offer results in a valid contract. 2. Offer must be made with objectively ascertained intention to be bound 8.2.3 Whether any particular statement amounts to an offer depends on the intention with which it is made. An offer must be made with the intention to be bound. On the other hand, if a person is merely soliciting offers or requesting for information, without any intention to be bound, at best, he or she would be making an invitation to treat. Under the objective test, a person may be said to have made an offer if his or her statement (or conduct) induces a reas
perturbation.justification: The modification removes liability for the recipient even if their breach causes information to become public, thus violating the principle that a breaching party is liable for damages. The 'Law Explanation' states this directly. Scraped Law Snippet 2 reinforces this by mentioning 'Remedies for Breach of Contract' and that Singapore contract law is based on English common law, which allows for damages for breach of contract.

perturbation.explanation: Modifying the agreement to state that the Recipient may disclose the information to any other person with only oral consent, instead of written consent, from the discloser is not legally binding. Written consent is much more reliable and indisputable than oral consent.
perturbation.law_citation: Section 65 of the Singapore Evidence Act
perturbation.law_explanation: While Singapore law recognizes oral contracts, disputes over consent are easier to prove with written evidence. Oral consent can be challenged for being misinterpreted. Therefore, stipulating oral consent is a weaker standard of proof and can contradict legal principles emphasizing reliable evidence in disputes.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: evidence | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you evidence Evidence an item or information proffered to make the existence of a fact more or less probable. Evidence can take the form of testimony , documents , photographs, videos, voice recordings, DNA testing, or other tangible objects. Courts cannot admit all evidence, as evidence must be admissible under that jurisdiction’s rules of evidence (see below) in order to be presented to court. In federal court , evidence is governed by the Federal Rules of Evidence . A court may exclude evidence because it is not relevant , hearsay , or otherwise inadmissible . Rules of evidence are, as the name indicates, the rules by which a court determines what evidence is admissible at trial . In the U.S., federal courts follow the Federal Rules of Evidence , while state courts generally follow their own rules. See, for example California's evidence code, Indiana's evidence rules, or Washington's evidence rules. State rules of evidence are generally imposed by the state legislature upon the state courts. In establishing what evidence is admissible, many rules of evidence concentrate first on the relevancy of the offered evidence. See, for example Article IV of the Federal Rules of Evidence. The admissibility of hearsay and oral testimony are addressed under Article VIII of the Federal Rules of Evidence. Rules of evidence also allocate among the parties the burden of producing evidence and the burden of persuading the court. See, for example Article III of the Federal Rules of Evidence or Division 5 of the California Evidence Code. Privileges are addressed under Article V of the Federal Rules of Evidence as well as Division Eight of the California Evidence Code. Federal Material U.S. Constitution CRS Annotated Constitution Federal Rules Federal Rules of Evidence Federal Judicial Decisions U.S. Supreme Court Recent Decisions on Evidence State Material State Statutes State Statutes Dealing with Evidence State Judicial Decisions N.Y. Court of Appeals ​ Decisions on Evidence Appellate Decisions from Other States [Last reviewed in November of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex THE LEGAL PROCESS courts criminal law criminal procedure evidence wex articles wex definitions civil procedure courts and procedure criminal law and procedure Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modification from written consent to oral consent introduces a legal contradiction because oral consent is a weaker form of evidence compared to written consent. This directly impacts the enforceability of the confidentiality agreement. Scraped Law Snippet 1 highlights the importance of evidence in legal proceedings, stating, "Evidence [is] an item or information proffered to make the existence of a fact more or less probable. Evidence can take the form of testimony, documents, photographs, videos, voice recordings, DNA testing, or other tangible objects." While oral testimony can be a form of evidence, its reliability is often challenged, especially in complex legal disputes. Requiring written consent provides a more definitive and easily verifiable record, reducing ambiguity and potential legal challenges. Therefore, changing the requirement to oral consent weakens the legal protection offered by the original clause.

perturbation.original_text:
6.  Variations
Any variations to this Agreement are to be made in writing and signed by both the Discloser and the Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text:
6.  Variations
Any variations to this Agreement are to be made verbally between the Discloser and the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the agreement such that variations are to be made verbally directly contradicts common contract practice in Singapore.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singapore Contract Law - Requirement of Written Amendments
perturbation.law_explanation: Singapore contract law states that certain contracts, including those involving significant commercial agreements like NDAs, ideally require written amendments to prevent disputes over the terms of the agreement. Changing the clause to allow for oral amendments undermines the stability and enforceability of the contract.

perturbation.justification: The change from requiring written variations to allowing verbal variations directly contradicts common contract practice, particularly for significant commercial agreements like NDAs, as explained in the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, Snippet 2 indicates that Singapore contract law is largely based on English common law, and agreements are enforceable at law (Section 2). The perturbation explanation indicates that this undermines the stability and enforceability of the contract, satisfying the condition for 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction'.
